event: An angel waking the prophet Elijah in the desert and providing food
symbols: sleeping Elijah — exhaustion and despair of the prophet fleeing Jezebel
angel touching Elijah — divine compassion reaching the broken servant
red wings of the angel — the fiery zeal and heavenly origin of the messenger
desert landscape — the wilderness of despair where God meets His servant
food provision (implied) — the bread and water that sustain Elijah for forty days
description: A small panel painting showing the prophet Elijah lying asleep or collapsed on the ground in a barren desert landscape, wearing a red cloak over darker garments. An angel with striking red wings and a pale blue robe bends over him, gently touching or waking him. The background shows rocky, arid terrain with sparse vegetation and distant hills. Another small angelic figure is visible in the upper right. The palette is warm with earth tones, red accents, and soft blues.
theological_description: This scene depicts one of the most tender moments in the Old Testament — God's gentle care for His exhausted prophet Elijah. After his triumph over the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel, Elijah fled in terror from Queen Jezebel's death threat and collapsed under a broom bush in the desert, praying to die: 'I have had enough, LORD. Take my life' (1 Kings 19:4). Instead of rebuking His despairing servant, God sent an angel who touched him and said, 'Get up and eat' (1 Kings 19:5). The angel provided bread and water — food that sustained Elijah for forty days and forty nights until he reached Horeb, the mountain of God (1 Kings 19:8). This provision typologically foreshadows Christ's own forty days in the wilderness (Matthew 4:1-2) and the Eucharistic bread that sustains believers on their journey. The scene powerfully illustrates God's response to human brokenness: not condemnation but compassion, not distance but intimate presence. As the Psalmist declares: 'The LORD is close to the brokenhearted and saves those who are crushed in spirit' (Psalm 34:18). The angel's ministering touch anticipates the angels who ministered to Jesus after His temptation (Matthew 4:11).

scripture_refs.reason: The angel providing food and water to the exhausted Elijah in the desert — the direct narrative
The angel touching sleeping Elijah and saying 'Get up and eat'
The Lord is close to the brokenhearted — God's tender care for despairing Elijah
